input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The Physical Twin is a 1/10-scale teleoperated autonomous vehicle retrofitted with advanced sensor and control technology. Its main hardware layers consist of lower and upper chassis components and autonomy elements. The system operates within the Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) domain, where it processes sensor data to detect lead vehicles, compute desired speeds based on distance, and execute actuation commands. The resource-constrained nature of its on-board ECUs necessitates digital twinning for computation offloading, real-time monitoring, and remote management capabilities.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides three key services: (1) Data Monitoring, featuring a user interface for real-time visualization of vehicle state data, projected driving paths, and detected object tracks, alongside data saving and report generation; (2) Computation Offloading, where the ACC system's lead vehicle detection, speed calculation, and command generation are processed externally to alleviate on-board ECU constraints; and (3) Remote Management, enabling operators to remotely enable/disable ACC, set target speeds, and issue emergency braking commands, while developers utilize the system for troubleshooting via data collection and monitoring. These services support both operational and development use cases for the ADAS system.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2) serves as the middleware for integrating application modules. NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0 is utilized as the Software Development Kit (SDK). Application modules are developed using Python 3.8, C++, and MATLAB/Simulink.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The system requires 100 ms clock synchronization accuracy for safety-critical vehicular applications due to hardware limitations, ensuring coordination between devices for computation offloading.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin system is structured into three hierarchical entities (Physical Twin, Digital Twin, and User) each deployed on separate hardware components. The Physical Twin Entity handles data pre-processing, actuation, and device communication; the Digital Twin Entity manages data and control mechanisms; and the User Entity includes 3D Visualization and Control Panel components. All functional entities are implemented as individual ROS2 packages, each containing ROS2 nodes for specific functionality.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The architecture follows the ISO 23247 Reference Architecture with modifications, deploying each functional entity (Physical Twin, Digital Twin, User) on distinct hardware. Components are organized into ROS2 packages where each FE is implemented as a ROS2 node, facilitating system-wide coordination through ROS2 middleware.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Computational models include DBSCAN for clustering raw LiDAR point clouds and Kalman filtering for object tracking. Data sources consist of streams from LiDAR, cameras, and other sensors, managed via a SQL database that aggregates object tracks and ego vehicle state for persistent storage and analysis.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Physical actuators include a Vedder Electronic Speed Controller for converting desired speed into motor voltage commands and a steering servo for vehicle steering control. The lower-level chassis implements speed and steering actuation through precise voltage-based motor control and servo-based steering mechanisms.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensing infrastructure comprises a Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR for 2D range scanning and an Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera for stereo vision, providing real-time spatial data streams for environmental perception and object detection.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The system enforces real-time and high-fidelity computational reflections, with validation requirements including implementation of Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) per ISO 15622. Key fidelity constraints include maximum network latency of 100 ms (Requirement R7) and clock synchronization accuracy of 100 ms (Requirement R8) across all components.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Physical sensor data (LiDAR point clouds, vehicle state, motor, steering, servo) is transmitted via Wi-Fi UDP. Raw LiDAR point cloud data is processed by the Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking component, converting it into structured tracks represented as individual points on a 2D plane. The Data Translation component serializes all incoming data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions. Data types include point cloud data, vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands generated by the Controlling FE are serialized by the Data Translation component and transmitted over Wi-Fi UDP to the Actuation FE for execution on physical actuators. The Data Translation component handles serialization, message type alignment, and unit conversions for command data streams.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Internal communication between software components uses ROS2, while inter-entity communication occurs over Wi-Fi using UDP in the transport layer. Requirement R7 specifies a maximum network latency of 100 ms. Bandwidth requirements and redundancy mechanisms are not detailed in the document.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Adherence to ISO 23247:2021, which specifies a four-part framework: Part 1 (Overview and general principles), Part 2 (Reference architecture), Part 3 (Digital representation of physical manufacturing elements), and Part 4 (Information exchange). The reference architecture (Part 2) employs an entity-based model where the Observable Manufacturing Element (OME) represents physical entities (e.g., equipment, facilities, products), the Data Collection and Device Control Entity handles data acquisition and control of OMEs, the Digital Twin Entity provides a digital representation of OMEs, and the User Entity offers interfaces for interaction (e.g., monitoring data, control). Each entity comprises Functional Entities (FEs) for specific tasks like actuation, data transformation, and user interfacing. The standard does not dictate FEs' implementation, with OPC Unified Architecture cited as a possible protocol.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

Provides a comprehensive description of the three specific services: Data Monitoring (visualization, reporting), Computation Offloading (ACC logic external processing), and Remote Management (commands, user types). Matches the source details perfectly. Supported by: 'view, save, and generate reports of vehicle state data', 'offloading only the ACC system from the vehicle to the DT', 'enabling/disabling ACC, setting the desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command'.

The extraction accurately lists Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2) as the middleware and NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0 as the Software Development Kit (SDK). It correctly identifies the programming languages used (Python 3.8, C++, MATLAB/Simulink) and briefly mentions their specific roles (rapid prototyping, ROS2 node lifecycle management, complex systems). Supported by quotes: 'Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2) [32] as the middleware to integrate application modules.'; 'We use NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0 as the Software Development Kit (SDK) for programming the Jetson Orin Nano'; 'Application modules are implemented in Python 3.8 (rapid prototyping), C++ (ROS2 node lifecycle management), and MATLAB/Simulink (for more complex systems...)'

block_2_docs_retrieved:
coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extraction correctly identifies the 100 ms clock synchronization accuracy requirement and cites hardware limitations, supported by the text: 'Components within the DTS shall have clocks synchronized to an accuracy of 100 ms' and 'We require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations'. However, the extraction incorrectly states the requirement is 'due to... safety-critical vehicular applications', whereas the text clarifies that stricter standards are usually required for safety-critical applications, and the 100 ms figure is chosen here specifically because of hardware limitations. This is a slight misinterpretation of the causal link.

The extraction accurately describes the three hierarchical entities (Physical Twin, Digital Twin, User) and their deployment on separate hardware, supported by the text: 'Considering that each entity has been deployed on a different hardware component'. It correctly details the responsibilities of each entity (e.g., 'Data pre-processing, actuation... are organized into the Physical Twin Entity') and notes that functional entities are implemented as ROS2 packages: 'implement each FE as an individual ROS2 package'. The extraction is specific and comprehensive.

The extraction correctly states the architecture follows ISO 23247 with modifications, supported by the text: 'We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design phase'. It accurately describes the deployment on distinct hardware ('each entity has been deployed on a different hardware component') and the use of ROS2 packages for implementation ('implement each FE as an individual ROS2 package'). The extraction is well-grounded and detailed.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The provided text focuses on the internal architecture of the specific Digital Twin System (DTS), its components (ROS2, ISO 23247 entities), and internal communication requirements. It does not discuss integration with external systems, interoperability standards with other enterprise systems, or data exchange protocols outside the defined DTS. Therefore, the assessment that this characteristic is not in the document is correct.

block_3_docs_retrieved:
In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately identifies the computational models and data management based on the source. Table I explicitly states the 'Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking' component 'Clusters raw LiDAR point clouds via DBSCAN, then tracks using a Kalman filter.' It also lists the 'Data Storage' component which 'Gathers object tracks and ego vehicle state into a SQL database.' The data sources are confirmed in the background section: 'Streams of data from LiDAR, cameras, and other sensors are fused in real time.'

The description matches the source details precisely. The 'Physical Twin' section lists 'a Vedder Electronic Speed Controller' and notes the 'lower level chassis is responsible for controlling the steering and the speed of the vehicle.' Table I specifies the 'Actuation' component 'Converts desired speed into motor voltage commands.' The presence of a steering servo is supported by Figure 2 and the context of the lower-level chassis implementation.

Correctly identifies the specific sensor models: 'Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR' and 'Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera' as listed in the 'Physical Twin' section. It correctly references real-time data streams, supported by the text: 'Streams of data from LiDAR, cameras, and other sensors are fused in real time.' However, the specific descriptors '2D range scanning' and 'stereo vision' are not found in the provided text, representing minor unsupported additions, resulting in a score of 4 instead of 5.

The extraction is highly accurate and grounded in the requirements section. The text defines DTs as 'real-time and high-fidelity computational reflections.' Requirement R6 mandates the system 'shall implement ACC, as outlined in ISO 15622.' Requirement R7 specifies 'network latency of at most 100 ms' and Requirement R8 requires 'clocks synchronized to an accuracy of 100 ms.'

Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system. Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer. _1) Data Monitoring:_ To enable data monitoring, ACCrelevant data such as vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path are displayed in real-time on the 3D Visualization, as shown in Fig. 3a. block_4_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately describes the data flow and components, such as the Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking and Data Translation. It correctly states that Data Translation 'serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions'. However, it incorrectly specifies 'Wi-Fi UDP' as the protocol; the source text cuts off at 'using U' and does not confirm UDP. Additionally, while it lists data types like vehicle state and projected driving path, these are supported by the requirements section.

The extraction correctly identifies the flow of control commands from the Controlling FE to the Actuation FE and the role of the Data Translation component in serialization and alignment. Supported by the description of 'Actuation' in Table I and the 'Data Translation' section. However, like the previous characteristic, it assumes 'UDP' as the protocol which is not fully present in the text (text ends at 'using U').

The extraction is highly specific and well-supported by the source. It correctly identifies ROS2 for internal communication and Wi-Fi for inter-entity communication. It accurately cites Requirement R7 for the 100 ms latency limit. It also correctly notes that bandwidth and redundancy mechanisms are not detailed in the document.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The source text mentions specific hardware (NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano) and that entities are deployed on different hardware components, but it does not detail the 'deployment infrastructure' in terms of hosting platforms (cloud/edge/on-premise specifics), computational/storage requirements, scalability mechanisms, or availability/reliability assurance as defined in the characteristic description. Therefore, stating it is not in the document is correct.

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

block_5_judge.reasoning: The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The source documents focus on the architecture, case study, and implementation of a specific Adaptive Cruise Control DT, referencing ISO 23247 and its parts. While 'DTs are also used as a product lifecycle management tool in support of digital threads of vehicles' is mentioned in section II-B, there is no detailed description of lifecycle phases, development stages, representation types at different phases, transition management, or lifecycle governance approaches specific to the Digital Twin system described. Therefore, the claim that this characteristic is not in the document is accurate.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The source text discusses the 'Engineering' of the DT (Section IV) and the architectural design, but does not provide detailed methodologies for the engineering process (e.g., specific software development lifecycles beyond ROS2 implementation), quality assurance practices, testing/validation approaches, or version control strategies. The 'Background' mentions the standard defines a development framework, but the text itself does not elaborate on the specific process or evolution management of the DT in the case study beyond the architectural mapping. Thus, the absence of specific details on these processes makes the 'Not in Document' claim correct.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The source text describes services like 'Data Monitoring', 'Computation Offloading', and 'Remote Management' (Section III-A) and mentions 'Data Report Generation' in Table I. While these imply insights (e.g., 'tracking system performance', 'vehicle state data'), the text does not describe the specific types of insights generated (analytics capabilities), the decision-making processes, or confidence measures for recommendations. It focuses more on the architecture and data flow rather than the analytical or decision-support mechanisms. Therefore, the claim that detailed insights and decision-making characteristics are not present is supported.

The description accurately summarizes the ISO 23247 standard as presented in the source text. It correctly identifies the four parts of the standard ('Overview and general principles', 'Reference architecture', 'Digital representation of physical manufacturing elements', and 'Information exchange') as found in section II-A. It details the Reference Architecture (Part 2) entities: Observable Manufacturing Element (OME), Data Collection and Device Control Entity, Digital Twin Entity, and User Entity, with correct descriptions of their roles. It also correctly notes the use of Functional Entities (FEs) and the lack of implementation guidelines, citing OPC UA as an example. Quotes supporting this: 'The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4) Information exchange.'; 'The Observable Manufacturing Element (OME) is any item that has an observable physical presence or operation...'; 'The standard does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented. For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture (UA) of IEC 62541...'
